title: Medication Administration Basics
subtitle: Basic Nursing Care · the rights, routes, and safe practice
scenario: Applying the rights of medication administration and safe practice within the LPT scope.

steps.q: Which of the following is one of the "rights" of safe medication administration?

opts.text: Right insurance plan
opts.rat: Insurance is a billing concern, not a medication-safety right.

opts.text: Right route
opts.rat: The rights include right client, drug, dose, route, time, and documentation.

opts.text: Right manufacturer
opts.rat: The manufacturer is not one of the medication rights.

opts.text: Right diagnosis code
opts.rat: A billing/diagnosis code is not one of the administration rights.

steps.q: Before giving a medication, the technician should identify the client by:

opts.text: Room and bed number
opts.rat: Clients move and beds change; location is not a reliable identifier.

opts.text: Asking another staff member who the client is
opts.rat: This is indirect and error-prone.

opts.text: Using two identifiers, such as name and date of birth, checked against the MAR
opts.rat: Two client identifiers verified against the medication record prevent wrong-client errors (a National Patient Safety Goal).

opts.text: Recognizing the client from previous shifts
opts.rat: Memory is unreliable and does not meet the two-identifier standard.

steps.q: Which medication task is WITHIN the LPT scope of practice?

opts.text: Titrating an IV vasoactive drip
opts.rat: Titrating vasoactive drips is an RN function.

opts.text: Administering a prescribed oral or intramuscular psychiatric medication
opts.rat: LPTs administer prescribed psychiatric medications by routes such as PO, IM, and SQ.

opts.text: Independently changing a client’s medication dose
opts.rat: Changing a dose requires a prescriber order; it is outside LPT scope.

opts.text: Pushing an IV medication to stop a cardiac arrhythmia
opts.rat: IV push of cardiac medications is outside LPT scope.

steps.q: A client refuses a prescribed medication. The technician should:

opts.text: Hide the medication in the client’s food
opts.rat: Covert medication violates client rights and is generally unlawful without specific legal authorization.

opts.text: Tell the client they must take it or lose privileges
opts.rat: Coercion violates the client’s right to refuse treatment.

opts.text: Respect the refusal, document it, and notify the nurse or prescriber
opts.rat: Clients have the right to refuse; the technician documents the refusal and reports it so the team can follow up.

opts.text: Give the medication anyway since it was ordered
opts.rat: Administering over a competent client’s refusal is battery.

steps.q: If a tablet is dropped on the floor during preparation, the technician should:

opts.text: Rinse it off and give it to the client
opts.rat: A dropped medication is considered contaminated.

opts.text: Discard it and obtain a new dose
opts.rat: A dropped tablet is contaminated and must be discarded; obtain a fresh dose and document per policy.

opts.text: Give it if the floor was recently cleaned
opts.rat: Floor cleanliness does not make a dropped dose acceptable.

opts.text: Return it to the stock bottle
opts.rat: Never return a dropped or removed medication to stock.

steps.q: On the medication record, the abbreviation "BID" means the medication is given:

opts.text: Once a day
opts.rat: Once daily is "daily" or "QD" (now often written out).

opts.text: Twice a day
opts.rat: BID means twice a day.

opts.text: Three times a day
opts.rat: Three times a day is "TID".

opts.text: Every other day
opts.rat: Every other day is not "BID".

steps.q: Before administering a PRN (as-needed) medication, the technician should FIRST:

opts.text: Give it at the same time each day
opts.rat: PRN medications are given based on need, not a fixed schedule.

opts.text: Assess the client’s need and verify the time of the last dose
opts.rat: PRN administration requires assessing the indication and confirming enough time has elapsed since the last dose.

opts.text: Ask the client’s roommate whether it is needed
opts.rat: The assessment is based on the client, not others.

opts.text: Document that it was given before assessing
opts.rat: Documentation follows administration, and assessment comes first.

steps.q: If the technician is unsure about a medication order or dose, the safest action is to:

opts.text: Give the dose that seems most reasonable
opts.rat: Guessing a dose is unsafe and outside safe practice.

opts.text: Skip the medication without telling anyone
opts.rat: Silently omitting a dose can harm the client and violates documentation duties.

opts.text: Clarify with the nurse or prescriber before administering
opts.rat: Uncertainty must be resolved by clarifying the order before giving the medication (CLAUDE.md §1: uncertainty = do not guess).

opts.text: Ask another client what they would do
opts.rat: Clients are not a source for clarifying medication orders.
